Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours, but then I'd get distracted by other things—work, hanging out with friends, or just scrolling on my phone. When I did sit down to study, I preferred watching videos or looking at diagrams since I'm pretty visual, but it was never consistent. I'd cram when things got urgent, but that was about it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I didn't have set study times—it really depended on whether I had a shift at work or if something was due soon. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework, and then other weeks I'd try to catch up on everything at once. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving, and even then, I often missed them.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got an email reminder about something being due or when I felt guilty about not checking in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, but then I'd feel overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind and log out again. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in a few times because they seemed less intimidating than the regular assignments.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit to check the LMS every morning, but that fell apart pretty quickly. Once I started missing classes and falling behind, logging in just reminded me of everything I wasn't doing. It never became automatic for me the way I think it was supposed to.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me because I could actually see what was being explained rather than just reading walls of text. I liked when there were diagrams or visual breakdowns of concepts. The gamified tasks were also kind of cool—they made things feel less like a chore and more like something I could actually complete without stressing out too much.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and the longer written assignments. They just felt like too much, especially when I was already behind. Discussion posts were something I'd skim but rarely contribute to because I didn't feel like I had anything smart to say when I hadn't been keeping up. I knew they were there, but I kept putting them off.
Q7: If I could finish something quickly and see immediate progress, it felt worth my time. The gamified stuff was good for that—I could see points adding up and it gave me a little boost. Long assignments that required me to have followed along with weeks of content? Those felt pointless to even start because I knew I'd just be lost.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt way less stressful. I'd actually look at optional materials sometimes because there was no pressure. Required activities made me anxious because I knew I was supposed to do them and wasn't, so I'd avoid them even more. It's kind of backwards, but the pressure made me shut down rather than step up.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos and then study from those on my phone or laptop without being connected to the LMS. I also used YouTube a lot to find explanations of concepts that I didn't understand from the course materials. Sometimes I'd take notes by hand from videos I'd already downloaded.
Q10: There were times when I'd spend hours going over downloaded slides or rewatching videos I'd saved, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with a friend who was in the same course, and we'd quiz each other, but that kind of studying doesn't leave a digital footprint. The LMS only knows what I did when I was logged in.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd open a video or download a PDF once, and then I'd study from it offline for days. It was easier than logging in every time, especially when my internet was spotty or I was trying to save data. The LMS probably thought I only looked at something once when I actually used it multiple times.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I was working more hours to pay rent, and I just couldn't keep up with nine online courses. It felt like I was drowning, and instead of asking for help, I just kind of checked out. The surprise of how far behind I'd gotten made it even harder to re-engage.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and face all my incomplete tasks. Confusion was a big one too—if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it instead of asking questions. It was easier to just not deal with it.
Q14: Yeah, there were plenty of times I'd log in, click through some stuff, maybe watch part of a video, but my brain wasn't really there. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It looked like activity on paper, but I wasn't learning—I was just present.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important, but I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would become next week. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed, and part of it was just not believing I could do well anyway. Avoidance became my default mode, even though I knew it was making things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really got me to act. I'd ignore everything until the last minute and then panic-submit something or just miss it entirely. The LMS would show a spike in my activity right before due dates, but it wasn't productive engagement—it was just desperation.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback because I wasn't submitting much work. When I did get feedback, it was usually too late for me to do anything about it. Honestly, feedback might have helped if I'd been more engaged from the start, but by the time I saw it, I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The structure was pretty overwhelming with nine courses running at once. I couldn't keep track of what was due when, and the LMS didn't make it easy to see the big picture. I needed more visual organization—like a dashboard that showed me exactly what I was missing and what was coming up.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller pieces and gave me instant feedback. But the sheer volume of content and the lack of flexibility in deadlines made it harder. Once I fell behind, there was no way to catch up without sacrificing everything else.
Q20: That was kind of me, honestly. I'd log in, click around, watch videos, participate in discussions a bit—but when it came to actually submitting work, I'd freeze. It's like the final step of putting something out there for judgment was too much. The activity looks good, but the output doesn't match because the pressure to perform was paralyzing.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone's studying offline or using external resources, they might not show up much in the LMS but still know the material. For me, my low activity matched my low performance, but I know people who barely logged in and still did fine because they learned differently.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and how long you spent on things. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention, if you understood anything, or if you were studying offline. They miss the human side—the stress, the confusion, the life stuff that affects how you engage.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in consistent effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and motivated to understand. For me, I wanted to be engaged, but life kept getting in the way, and I couldn't sustain it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually do care about learning, even if my numbers look bad. It wouldn't show the hours I spent stressing about falling behind or the offline studying I did that never got recorded. It also wouldn't show how much my work schedule and financial stress affected my ability to keep up. There's a whole person behind those clicks that the data just doesn't capture.
